<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sugawara (Klan)]][[en:Sugawara clan]][[fr:Clan Sugawara]][[ja:菅原氏]][[ru:Сугавара (род)]][[uk:Рід Суґавара]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sugawara (Klan)]][[en:Sugawara clan]][[fr:Clan Sugawara]][[ja:菅原氏]][[ru:Сугавара (род)]][[uk:Рід Суґавара]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sugawara (Klan)]][[en:Sugawara clan]][[fr:Clan Sugawara]][[ja:菅原氏]][[ru:Сугавара (род)]][[uk:Рід Суґавара]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1771125]] Wikidata name: Sugawara clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese aristocratic family}} {{family name hatnote|Sugawara|lang=Japanese}} {{Japanese clan name |surname=Sugawara clan |surname nihongo=菅原氏 |image=Japanese_crest_Umebachi.svg |caption=[[:en:Mon (badge)|''Mon'']]: ''Umebachi'' |province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] |parent house=[[Haji clan]] |founder=[[Sugawara no Furuhito]] |founding year=781 |titles= |ruled until= |dissolution= |cadet branches={{Plainlist | * [[Takatsuji clan]] * [[Gojō clan]] * [[Higashibōjō clan]] * [[Karahashi clan]] * [[Kiyo'oka clan]] * [[Kuwahara clan]] }}}} The '''Sugawara clan''' (菅原氏, ''Sugawara-uji'') was a Japanese aristocratic family claiming descent from [[Ame no Hohi]]. Founded in 781, they served the [[:en:Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court]] as scholars and government officials since the clan's foundation until the early modern period when the clan divided into six branches.
In the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] served as [[:en:Minister of the Right|Minister of the Right]], but the clan weakened soon after being demoted from the upper court nobility, ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]''. They continued to serve as heads of the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Imperial University]], until Sugawara no Tamenaga became [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Associate Counselor]], after which the clan reentered the upper court nobility. The clan later divided into six branches, the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], [[Kiyo'oka clan]] and [[Kuwahara clan]], all of which stayed in upper court nobility.
== Origins == [[File:Sugawara no Furuhito.jpg|thumb|[[Sugawara no Furuhito]], founder of the Sugawara clan]] The clan ancestor, [[Nomi no Sukune]], claimed descent from the male deity [[Ame no Hohi]]. Sukune belonged to the [[Haji clan]] that was in charge of burial rites. In 781, [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] became the first person to take the surname Sugawara, and thus founded the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Takigawa |first=Kōji |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1125133338 |title=Sugawarano Michizane : gakusha seijika no eikō to botsuraku |last2=滝川 |first2=幸司 |year=2019 |isbn=978-4-12-102559-3 |pages=1–2 |oclc=1125133338}}</ref> The name was taken from the Sugawara village in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]].
== History == Sugawara no Furuhito studied [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] and became a personal scholar (''jidoku'') to [[Emperor Kanmu]] (r. 781–806).<ref name=":0" /> Furuhito's son [[:en:Sugawara no Kiyotomo|Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] (770-842) held the [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] of [[Junior Third Rank]], and his son [[:en:Sugawara no Koreyoshi|Sugawara no Koreyoshi]] (812-880) was also of the same Rank and served as [[:en:Sangi (Japan)|Associate Counselor]]. Both Kiyotomo and Koreyoshi were scholars who served as chief experts on literature at the [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Imperial University]]. Kiyotomo also founded a private school called Kanke Rōka, educated many students, and established ''kidendō'', the study of history, as the family business.
[[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] (845-903) was promoted to [[Junior Second Rank]] and [[:en:Minister of the Right|Minister of the Right]] by [[:en:Emperor Uda|Emperor Uda]]. However, in 901, he was demoted to Provisional Governor-General of [[:en:Dazaifu (government)|Dazaifu]] (''Dazai no Gon-no-Sochi''), and died there. Michizane's sons were also disfavored, but in 906, his son and heir apparent Sugawara no Takami (876-913) was pardoned, and he was made head of the Imperial University.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=滝川 |first=幸司 |date=2017-09-30 |title=Sugawara no Michizaneの子息をめぐる二、三の問題 : 阿視と高視・淳茂の左遷その他 |url=https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050001337578644096 |journal=女子大國文 |language=ja |volume=161 |pages=21–56}}</ref> The descendants of Takami continued to serve as heads of the Imperial University as well as provincial governors. However, apart from [[Sugawara no Fumitoki]] (899-981), who was Junior Third Rank, and Sugawara no Sukemasa (925-1009), who was Junior Third Rank, Associate Counselor and Undersecretary of Dazaifu (''Dazai no Daini''), the clan members were not part of the upper court nobility, ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]''. Another notable member of the clan during this period was [[:en:Takasue's daughter|Sugawara no Takasue's daughter]], the author of ''[[:en:Sarashina Nikki|Sarashina Nikki]]''.
In the early [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], Sugawara no Tamenaga (1158-1246) rose up to become [[Senior Second Rank]], Associate Counselor and Head of the [[:en:Ministry of the Treasury|Ministry of the Treasury]] (''Ōkura-kyō''). Since then, the Sugawara clan belonged to the upper court nobility for generations. Additionally, because the [[Ōe clan]], that had produced many chief experts on literature, had weakened, the Sugawara dominated the posts within the Faculty of Japanese and Chinese History.<ref name=":1" />
In the early modern period, the clan divided into six branches: the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], Kiyo'oka clan and Kuwahara clan, all of which were ''dōjōke''-Ranking (''hanke'') ''[[kuge]]'' families. After the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the majority of the Confucian scholars participating in the planning process of [[:en:Japanese era name|era names]] were descendants of the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Tokoro |first=Isao |title=「日本年号の来歴と特色」第106回歴博フォーラム 年号と日本文化 -国立歴史民俗博物館 |url=https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/events/forum/old/f2017/pdf/106.pdf |access-date=2022-06-02 |website=National Museum of Japanese History |page=5}}</ref>
In the medieval [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] society, the Karahashi clan was considered to be the main line of the Sugawara clan, and dominated the role of Sugawara clan leader known as ''Kitano no Chōja''. However, after the Karahashi Arikazu murder incident, the clan weakened, and members of the Takatsuji clan, Gojō clan and Higashibōjō clan were chosen for the role instead.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Itō |first=Shingo |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/780474814 |title=Muromachi Sengokuki no kuge shakai to bunji |last2=伊藤慎吾 |publisher=Miyai Shoten |year=2012 |isbn=978-4-8382-3218-5 |edition=Shohan |pages=28–29 |oclc=780474814}}</ref>
== Clans descended from Sugawara ==
* [[Hisamatsu clan]]; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=Hotta |first=Masaatsu |date=1923 |title=寛政重脩諸家譜. 第6輯 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1082716 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |pages=889, 915, 933 |language=ja}}</ref> * [[Mimasaka Kan clan]]; a samurai family from [[:en:Mimasaka Province|Mimasaka Province]].<ref name=":2" /> * [[Maeda clan]]; descended from the Harada clan, a branch of the Mimasaka Kan clan, or from a son born to Sugawara no Michizane in [[Kyushu]].<ref name=":2" /> * Minobe family; a [[:en:Kōga-ryū|Kōga-ryū]] family that claims descent from Sugawara no Atsushige.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kido |first=Masayuki |date=2009 |title=城に見る水口の中世から近世 ―美濃部氏城館・水口岡山城・水口城― |url=http://www.shiga-bunkazai.jp/download/kiyou/22_kido.pdf |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Shiga Prefectural Association for Cultural Heritage |page=2}}</ref> * [[:en:Yagyū clan|Yagyū clan]]; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Arai |first=Hakuseki |date=1894 |title=藩翰譜. 巻3 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1142642 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |publisher=Yoshikawa Hanshichi |page=35 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable members ==
* [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] * [[:en:Sugawara no Kiyotomo|Sugawara no Kiyotomo]] * [[:en:Sugawara no Koreyoshi|Sugawara no Koreyoshi]] * [[:en:Sugawara no Michizane|Sugawara no Michizane]] * [[:en:Takasue's daughter|Sugawara no Takasue's daughter]]
== See also ==
* [[:en:Daigaku-ryō|Daigaku-ryō]] * [[Takano no Niigasa]] (Niece of Nomi no Sukune)
== References ==

Founded in 781, they served the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|WD=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court}} as scholars and government officials since the clan's foundation until the early modern period when the clan divided into six branches.
In the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} served as {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}}, but the clan weakened soon after being demoted from the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. They continued to serve as heads of the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}, until Sugawara no Tamenaga became {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor}}, after which the clan reentered the upper court nobility. The clan later divided into six branches, the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], [[Kiyo'oka clan]] and [[Kuwahara clan]], all of which stayed in upper court nobility.
== Origins == [[File:Sugawara no Furuhito.jpg|thumb|[[Sugawara no Furuhito]], founder of the Sugawara clan]] The clan ancestor, [[Nomi no Sukune]], claimed descent from the male deity [[Ame no Hohi]]. Sukune belonged to the [[Haji clan]] that was in charge of burial rites. In 781, [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] became the first person to take the surname Sugawara, and thus founded the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Takigawa |first=Kōji |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1125133338 |title=Sugawarano Michizane : gakusha seijika no eikō to botsuraku |last2=滝川 |first2=幸司 |year=2019 |isbn=978-4-12-102559-3 |pages=1–2 |oclc=1125133338}}</ref> The name was taken from the Sugawara village in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.
== History == Sugawara no Furuhito studied {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}} and became a personal scholar (''jidoku'') to [[Emperor Kanmu]] (r. 781–806).<ref name=":0" /> Furuhito's son {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} (770-842) held the [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] of [[Junior Third Rank]], and his son {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} (812-880) was also of the same Rank and served as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor}}. Both Kiyotomo and Koreyoshi were scholars who served as chief experts on literature at the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}. Kiyotomo also founded a private school called Kanke Rōka, educated many students, and established ''kidendō'', the study of history, as the family business.
{{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} (845-903) was promoted to [[Junior Second Rank]] and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda}}. However, in 901, he was demoted to Provisional Governor-General of {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu}} (''Dazai no Gon-no-Sochi''), and died there. Michizane's sons were also disfavored, but in 906, his son and heir apparent Sugawara no Takami (876-913) was pardoned, and he was made head of the Imperial University.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=滝川 |first=幸司 |date=2017-09-30 |title=Sugawara no Michizaneの子息をめぐる二、三の問題 : 阿視と高視・淳茂の左遷その他 |url=https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050001337578644096 |journal=女子大國文 |language=ja |volume=161 |pages=21–56}}</ref> The descendants of Takami continued to serve as heads of the Imperial University as well as provincial governors. However, apart from [[Sugawara no Fumitoki]] (899-981), who was Junior Third Rank, and Sugawara no Sukemasa (925-1009), who was Junior Third Rank, Associate Counselor and Undersecretary of Dazaifu (''Dazai no Daini''), the clan members were not part of the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. Another notable member of the clan during this period was {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}, the author of ''{{ill|Sarashina Nikki|en|Sarashina Nikki|WD=Q217955|lt=Sarashina Nikki}}''.
In the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Sugawara no Tamenaga (1158-1246) rose up to become [[Senior Second Rank]], Associate Counselor and Head of the {{ill|Ministry of the Treasury|en|Ministry of the Treasury|WD=Q112323848|lt=Ministry of the Treasury}} (''Ōkura-kyō''). Since then, the Sugawara clan belonged to the upper court nobility for generations. Additionally, because the [[Ōe clan]], that had produced many chief experts on literature, had weakened, the Sugawara dominated the posts within the Faculty of Japanese and Chinese History.<ref name=":1" />
In the early modern period, the clan divided into six branches: the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], Kiyo'oka clan and Kuwahara clan, all of which were ''dōjōke''-Ranking (''hanke'') ''[[kuge]]'' families. After the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the majority of the Confucian scholars participating in the planning process of {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=era names}} were descendants of the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Tokoro |first=Isao |title=「日本年号の来歴と特色」第106回歴博フォーラム 年号と日本文化 -国立歴史民俗博物館 |url=https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/events/forum/old/f2017/pdf/106.pdf |access-date=2022-06-02 |website=National Museum of Japanese History |page=5}}</ref>
In the medieval {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} society, the Karahashi clan was considered to be the main line of the Sugawara clan, and dominated the role of Sugawara clan leader known as ''Kitano no Chōja''. However, after the Karahashi Arikazu murder incident, the clan weakened, and members of the Takatsuji clan, Gojō clan and Higashibōjō clan were chosen for the role instead.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Itō |first=Shingo |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/780474814 |title=Muromachi Sengokuki no kuge shakai to bunji |last2=伊藤慎吾 |publisher=Miyai Shoten |year=2012 |isbn=978-4-8382-3218-5 |edition=Shohan |pages=28–29 |oclc=780474814}}</ref>
== Clans descended from Sugawara ==
* [[Hisamatsu clan]]; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=Hotta |first=Masaatsu |date=1923 |title=寛政重脩諸家譜. 第6輯 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1082716 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |pages=889, 915, 933 |language=ja}}</ref> * [[Mimasaka Kan clan]]; a samurai family from {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}.<ref name=":2" /> * [[Maeda clan]]; descended from the Harada clan, a branch of the Mimasaka Kan clan, or from a son born to Sugawara no Michizane in [[Kyushu]].<ref name=":2" /> * Minobe family; a {{ill|Kōga-ryū|en|Kōga-ryū|WD=Q6455112|lt=Kōga-ryū}} family that claims descent from Sugawara no Atsushige.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kido |first=Masayuki |date=2009 |title=城に見る水口の中世から近世 ―美濃部氏城館・水口岡山城・水口城― |url=http://www.shiga-bunkazai.jp/download/kiyou/22_kido.pdf |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Shiga Prefectural Association for Cultural Heritage |page=2}}</ref> * {{ill|Yagyū clan|en|Yagyū clan|WD=Q2975395|lt=Yagyū clan}}; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Arai |first=Hakuseki |date=1894 |title=藩翰譜. 巻3 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1142642 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |publisher=Yoshikawa Hanshichi |page=35 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable members ==

In the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} served as {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}}, but the clan weakened soon after being demoted from the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. They continued to serve as heads of the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}, until Sugawara no Tamenaga became {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor}}, after which the clan reentered the upper court nobility. The clan later divided into six branches, the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], [[Kiyo'oka clan]] and [[Kuwahara clan]], all of which stayed in upper court nobility.
== Origins == [[File:Sugawara no Furuhito.jpg|thumb|[[Sugawara no Furuhito]], founder of the Sugawara clan]] The clan ancestor, [[Nomi no Sukune]], claimed descent from the male deity [[Ame no Hohi]]. Sukune belonged to the [[Haji clan]] that was in charge of burial rites. In 781, [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] became the first person to take the surname Sugawara, and thus founded the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Takigawa |first=Kōji |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1125133338 |title=Sugawarano Michizane : gakusha seijika no eikō to botsuraku |last2=滝川 |first2=幸司 |year=2019 |isbn=978-4-12-102559-3 |pages=1–2 |oclc=1125133338}}</ref> The name was taken from the Sugawara village in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.
== History == Sugawara no Furuhito studied {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}} and became a personal scholar (''jidoku'') to [[Emperor Kanmu]] (r. 781–806).<ref name=":0" /> Furuhito's son {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} (770-842) held the [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] of [[Junior Third Rank]], and his son {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} (812-880) was also of the same Rank and served as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor}}. Both Kiyotomo and Koreyoshi were scholars who served as chief experts on literature at the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}. Kiyotomo also founded a private school called Kanke Rōka, educated many students, and established ''kidendō'', the study of history, as the family business.
{{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} (845-903) was promoted to [[Junior Second Rank]] and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda}}. However, in 901, he was demoted to Provisional Governor-General of {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu}} (''Dazai no Gon-no-Sochi''), and died there. Michizane's sons were also disfavored, but in 906, his son and heir apparent Sugawara no Takami (876-913) was pardoned, and he was made head of the Imperial University.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=滝川 |first=幸司 |date=2017-09-30 |title=Sugawara no Michizaneの子息をめぐる二、三の問題 : 阿視と高視・淳茂の左遷その他 |url=https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050001337578644096 |journal=女子大國文 |language=ja |volume=161 |pages=21–56}}</ref> The descendants of Takami continued to serve as heads of the Imperial University as well as provincial governors. However, apart from [[Sugawara no Fumitoki]] (899-981), who was Junior Third Rank, and Sugawara no Sukemasa (925-1009), who was Junior Third Rank, Associate Counselor and Undersecretary of Dazaifu (''Dazai no Daini''), the clan members were not part of the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. Another notable member of the clan during this period was {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}, the author of ''{{ill|Sarashina Nikki|en|Sarashina Nikki|WD=Q217955|lt=Sarashina Nikki}}''.
In the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Sugawara no Tamenaga (1158-1246) rose up to become [[Senior Second Rank]], Associate Counselor and Head of the {{ill|Ministry of the Treasury|en|Ministry of the Treasury|WD=Q112323848|lt=Ministry of the Treasury}} (''Ōkura-kyō''). Since then, the Sugawara clan belonged to the upper court nobility for generations. Additionally, because the [[Ōe clan]], that had produced many chief experts on literature, had weakened, the Sugawara dominated the posts within the Faculty of Japanese and Chinese History.<ref name=":1" />
In the early modern period, the clan divided into six branches: the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], Kiyo'oka clan and Kuwahara clan, all of which were ''dōjōke''-Ranking (''hanke'') ''[[kuge]]'' families. After the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the majority of the Confucian scholars participating in the planning process of {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=era names}} were descendants of the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Tokoro |first=Isao |title=「日本年号の来歴と特色」第106回歴博フォーラム 年号と日本文化 -国立歴史民俗博物館 |url=https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/events/forum/old/f2017/pdf/106.pdf |access-date=2022-06-02 |website=National Museum of Japanese History |page=5}}</ref>
In the medieval {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} society, the Karahashi clan was considered to be the main line of the Sugawara clan, and dominated the role of Sugawara clan leader known as ''Kitano no Chōja''. However, after the Karahashi Arikazu murder incident, the clan weakened, and members of the Takatsuji clan, Gojō clan and Higashibōjō clan were chosen for the role instead.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Itō |first=Shingo |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/780474814 |title=Muromachi Sengokuki no kuge shakai to bunji |last2=伊藤慎吾 |publisher=Miyai Shoten |year=2012 |isbn=978-4-8382-3218-5 |edition=Shohan |pages=28–29 |oclc=780474814}}</ref>
== Clans descended from Sugawara ==
* [[Hisamatsu clan]]; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=Hotta |first=Masaatsu |date=1923 |title=寛政重脩諸家譜. 第6輯 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1082716 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |pages=889, 915, 933 |language=ja}}</ref> * [[Mimasaka Kan clan]]; a samurai family from {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}.<ref name=":2" /> * [[Maeda clan]]; descended from the Harada clan, a branch of the Mimasaka Kan clan, or from a son born to Sugawara no Michizane in [[Kyushu]].<ref name=":2" /> * Minobe family; a {{ill|Kōga-ryū|en|Kōga-ryū|WD=Q6455112|lt=Kōga-ryū}} family that claims descent from Sugawara no Atsushige.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kido |first=Masayuki |date=2009 |title=城に見る水口の中世から近世 ―美濃部氏城館・水口岡山城・水口城― |url=http://www.shiga-bunkazai.jp/download/kiyou/22_kido.pdf |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Shiga Prefectural Association for Cultural Heritage |page=2}}</ref> * {{ill|Yagyū clan|en|Yagyū clan|WD=Q2975395|lt=Yagyū clan}}; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Arai |first=Hakuseki |date=1894 |title=藩翰譜. 巻3 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1142642 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |publisher=Yoshikawa Hanshichi |page=35 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable members ==
* [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}
== See also ==
* {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}} * [[Takano no Niigasa]] (Niece of Nomi no Sukune)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sugawara (Klan)]][[en:Sugawara clan]][[fr:Clan Sugawara]][[ja:菅原氏]][[ru:Сугавара (род)]][[uk:Рід Суґавара]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sugawara (Klan)]][[en:Sugawara clan]][[fr:Clan Sugawara]][[ja:菅原氏]][[ru:Сугавара (род)]][[uk:Рід Суґавара]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sugawara (Klan)]][[en:Sugawara clan]][[fr:Clan Sugawara]][[ja:菅原氏]][[ru:Сугавара (род)]][[uk:Рід Суґавара]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1771125]] Wikidata name: Sugawara clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese aristocratic family}} {{family name hatnote|Sugawara|lang=Japanese}} {{Japanese clan name |surname=Sugawara clan |surname nihongo=菅原氏 |image=Japanese_crest_Umebachi.svg |caption=[[:en:Mon (badge)|''Mon'']]: ''Umebachi'' |province of origin={{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} |parent house=[[Haji clan]] |founder=[[Sugawara no Furuhito]] |founding year=781 |titles= |ruled until= |dissolution= |cadet branches={{Plainlist | * [[Takatsuji clan]] * [[Gojō clan]] * [[Higashibōjō clan]] * [[Karahashi clan]] * [[Kiyo'oka clan]] * [[Kuwahara clan]] }}}} The '''Sugawara clan''' (菅原氏, ''Sugawara-uji'') was a Japanese aristocratic family claiming descent from [[Ame no Hohi]]. Founded in 781, they served the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|WD=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court}} as scholars and government officials since the clan's foundation until the early modern period when the clan divided into six branches.
In the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} served as {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}}, but the clan weakened soon after being demoted from the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. They continued to serve as heads of the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}, until Sugawara no Tamenaga became {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor}}, after which the clan reentered the upper court nobility. The clan later divided into six branches, the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], [[Kiyo'oka clan]] and [[Kuwahara clan]], all of which stayed in upper court nobility.
== Origins == [[File:Sugawara no Furuhito.jpg|thumb|[[Sugawara no Furuhito]], founder of the Sugawara clan]] The clan ancestor, [[Nomi no Sukune]], claimed descent from the male deity [[Ame no Hohi]]. Sukune belonged to the [[Haji clan]] that was in charge of burial rites. In 781, [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] became the first person to take the surname Sugawara, and thus founded the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Takigawa |first=Kōji |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1125133338 |title=Sugawarano Michizane : gakusha seijika no eikō to botsuraku |last2=滝川 |first2=幸司 |year=2019 |isbn=978-4-12-102559-3 |pages=1–2 |oclc=1125133338}}</ref> The name was taken from the Sugawara village in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.

Both Kiyotomo and Koreyoshi were scholars who served as chief experts on literature at the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}. Kiyotomo also founded a private school called Kanke Rōka, educated many students, and established ''kidendō'', the study of history, as the family business.
{{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} (845-903) was promoted to [[Junior Second Rank]] and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda}}. However, in 901, he was demoted to Provisional Governor-General of {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu}} (''Dazai no Gon-no-Sochi''), and died there. Michizane's sons were also disfavored, but in 906, his son and heir apparent Sugawara no Takami (876-913) was pardoned, and he was made head of the Imperial University.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=滝川 |first=幸司 |date=2017-09-30 |title=Sugawara no Michizaneの子息をめぐる二、三の問題 : 阿視と高視・淳茂の左遷その他 |url=https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050001337578644096 |journal=女子大國文 |language=ja |volume=161 |pages=21–56}}</ref> The descendants of Takami continued to serve as heads of the Imperial University as well as provincial governors. However, apart from [[Sugawara no Fumitoki]] (899-981), who was Junior Third Rank, and Sugawara no Sukemasa (925-1009), who was Junior Third Rank, Associate Counselor and Undersecretary of Dazaifu (''Dazai no Daini''), the clan members were not part of the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. Another notable member of the clan during this period was {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}, the author of ''{{ill|Sarashina Nikki|en|Sarashina Nikki|WD=Q217955|lt=Sarashina Nikki}}''.
In the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Sugawara no Tamenaga (1158-1246) rose up to become [[Senior Second Rank]], Associate Counselor and Head of the {{ill|Ministry of the Treasury|en|Ministry of the Treasury|WD=Q112323848|lt=Ministry of the Treasury}} (''Ōkura-kyō''). Since then, the Sugawara clan belonged to the upper court nobility for generations. Additionally, because the [[Ōe clan]], that had produced many chief experts on literature, had weakened, the Sugawara dominated the posts within the Faculty of Japanese and Chinese History.<ref name=":1" />

After the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the majority of the Confucian scholars participating in the planning process of {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=era names}} were descendants of the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Tokoro |first=Isao |title=「日本年号の来歴と特色」第106回歴博フォーラム 年号と日本文化 -国立歴史民俗博物館 |url=https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/events/forum/old/f2017/pdf/106.pdf |access-date=2022-06-02 |website=National Museum of Japanese History |page=5}}</ref>
In the medieval {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} society, the Karahashi clan was considered to be the main line of the Sugawara clan, and dominated the role of Sugawara clan leader known as ''Kitano no Chōja''. However, after the Karahashi Arikazu murder incident, the clan weakened, and members of the Takatsuji clan, Gojō clan and Higashibōjō clan were chosen for the role instead.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Itō |first=Shingo |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/780474814 |title=Muromachi Sengokuki no kuge shakai to bunji |last2=伊藤慎吾 |publisher=Miyai Shoten |year=2012 |isbn=978-4-8382-3218-5 |edition=Shohan |pages=28–29 |oclc=780474814}}</ref>
== Clans descended from Sugawara ==
* [[Hisamatsu clan]]; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=Hotta |first=Masaatsu |date=1923 |title=寛政重脩諸家譜. 第6輯 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1082716 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |pages=889, 915, 933 |language=ja}}</ref> * [[Mimasaka Kan clan]]; a samurai family from {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}.<ref name=":2" /> * [[Maeda clan]]; descended from the Harada clan, a branch of the Mimasaka Kan clan, or from a son born to Sugawara no Michizane in [[Kyushu]].<ref name=":2" /> * Minobe family; a {{ill|Kōga-ryū|en|Kōga-ryū|WD=Q6455112|lt=Kōga-ryū}} family that claims descent from Sugawara no Atsushige.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kido |first=Masayuki |date=2009 |title=城に見る水口の中世から近世 ―美濃部氏城館・水口岡山城・水口城― |url=http://www.shiga-bunkazai.jp/download/kiyou/22_kido.pdf |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Shiga Prefectural Association for Cultural Heritage |page=2}}</ref> * {{ill|Yagyū clan|en|Yagyū clan|WD=Q2975395|lt=Yagyū clan}}; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Arai |first=Hakuseki |date=1894 |title=藩翰譜. 巻3 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1142642 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |publisher=Yoshikawa Hanshichi |page=35 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable members ==

== Origins == [[File:Sugawara no Furuhito.jpg|thumb|[[Sugawara no Furuhito]], founder of the Sugawara clan]] The clan ancestor, [[Nomi no Sukune]], claimed descent from the male deity [[Ame no Hohi]]. Sukune belonged to the [[Haji clan]] that was in charge of burial rites. In 781, [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] became the first person to take the surname Sugawara, and thus founded the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Takigawa |first=Kōji |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1125133338 |title=Sugawarano Michizane : gakusha seijika no eikō to botsuraku |last2=滝川 |first2=幸司 |year=2019 |isbn=978-4-12-102559-3 |pages=1–2 |oclc=1125133338}}</ref> The name was taken from the Sugawara village in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.
== History == Sugawara no Furuhito studied {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}} and became a personal scholar (''jidoku'') to [[Emperor Kanmu]] (r. 781–806).<ref name=":0" /> Furuhito's son {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} (770-842) held the [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] of [[Junior Third Rank]], and his son {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} (812-880) was also of the same Rank and served as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor}}. Both Kiyotomo and Koreyoshi were scholars who served as chief experts on literature at the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}. Kiyotomo also founded a private school called Kanke Rōka, educated many students, and established ''kidendō'', the study of history, as the family business.
{{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} (845-903) was promoted to [[Junior Second Rank]] and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda}}. However, in 901, he was demoted to Provisional Governor-General of {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu}} (''Dazai no Gon-no-Sochi''), and died there. Michizane's sons were also disfavored, but in 906, his son and heir apparent Sugawara no Takami (876-913) was pardoned, and he was made head of the Imperial University.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=滝川 |first=幸司 |date=2017-09-30 |title=Sugawara no Michizaneの子息をめぐる二、三の問題 : 阿視と高視・淳茂の左遷その他 |url=https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050001337578644096 |journal=女子大國文 |language=ja |volume=161 |pages=21–56}}</ref> The descendants of Takami continued to serve as heads of the Imperial University as well as provincial governors. However, apart from [[Sugawara no Fumitoki]] (899-981), who was Junior Third Rank, and Sugawara no Sukemasa (925-1009), who was Junior Third Rank, Associate Counselor and Undersecretary of Dazaifu (''Dazai no Daini''), the clan members were not part of the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. Another notable member of the clan during this period was {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}, the author of ''{{ill|Sarashina Nikki|en|Sarashina Nikki|WD=Q217955|lt=Sarashina Nikki}}''.
In the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Sugawara no Tamenaga (1158-1246) rose up to become [[Senior Second Rank]], Associate Counselor and Head of the {{ill|Ministry of the Treasury|en|Ministry of the Treasury|WD=Q112323848|lt=Ministry of the Treasury}} (''Ōkura-kyō''). Since then, the Sugawara clan belonged to the upper court nobility for generations. Additionally, because the [[Ōe clan]], that had produced many chief experts on literature, had weakened, the Sugawara dominated the posts within the Faculty of Japanese and Chinese History.<ref name=":1" />
In the early modern period, the clan divided into six branches: the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], Kiyo'oka clan and Kuwahara clan, all of which were ''dōjōke''-Ranking (''hanke'') ''[[kuge]]'' families. After the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the majority of the Confucian scholars participating in the planning process of {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=era names}} were descendants of the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Tokoro |first=Isao |title=「日本年号の来歴と特色」第106回歴博フォーラム 年号と日本文化 -国立歴史民俗博物館 |url=https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/events/forum/old/f2017/pdf/106.pdf |access-date=2022-06-02 |website=National Museum of Japanese History |page=5}}</ref>
In the medieval {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} society, the Karahashi clan was considered to be the main line of the Sugawara clan, and dominated the role of Sugawara clan leader known as ''Kitano no Chōja''. However, after the Karahashi Arikazu murder incident, the clan weakened, and members of the Takatsuji clan, Gojō clan and Higashibōjō clan were chosen for the role instead.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Itō |first=Shingo |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/780474814 |title=Muromachi Sengokuki no kuge shakai to bunji |last2=伊藤慎吾 |publisher=Miyai Shoten |year=2012 |isbn=978-4-8382-3218-5 |edition=Shohan |pages=28–29 |oclc=780474814}}</ref>
== Clans descended from Sugawara ==
* [[Hisamatsu clan]]; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=Hotta |first=Masaatsu |date=1923 |title=寛政重脩諸家譜. 第6輯 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1082716 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |pages=889, 915, 933 |language=ja}}</ref> * [[Mimasaka Kan clan]]; a samurai family from {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}.<ref name=":2" /> * [[Maeda clan]]; descended from the Harada clan, a branch of the Mimasaka Kan clan, or from a son born to Sugawara no Michizane in [[Kyushu]].<ref name=":2" /> * Minobe family; a {{ill|Kōga-ryū|en|Kōga-ryū|WD=Q6455112|lt=Kōga-ryū}} family that claims descent from Sugawara no Atsushige.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kido |first=Masayuki |date=2009 |title=城に見る水口の中世から近世 ―美濃部氏城館・水口岡山城・水口城― |url=http://www.shiga-bunkazai.jp/download/kiyou/22_kido.pdf |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Shiga Prefectural Association for Cultural Heritage |page=2}}</ref> * {{ill|Yagyū clan|en|Yagyū clan|WD=Q2975395|lt=Yagyū clan}}; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Arai |first=Hakuseki |date=1894 |title=藩翰譜. 巻3 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1142642 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |publisher=Yoshikawa Hanshichi |page=35 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable members ==
* [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}
== See also ==
* {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}} * [[Takano no Niigasa]] (Niece of Nomi no Sukune)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sugawara (Klan)]][[en:Sugawara clan]][[fr:Clan Sugawara]][[ja:菅原氏]][[ru:Сугавара (род)]][[uk:Рід Суґавара]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sugawara (Klan)]][[en:Sugawara clan]][[fr:Clan Sugawara]][[ja:菅原氏]][[ru:Сугавара (род)]][[uk:Рід Суґавара]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Sugawara (Klan)]][[en:Sugawara clan]][[fr:Clan Sugawara]][[ja:菅原氏]][[ru:Сугавара (род)]][[uk:Рід Суґавара]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1771125]] Wikidata name: Sugawara clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese aristocratic family}} {{family name hatnote|Sugawara|lang=Japanese}} {{Japanese clan name |surname=Sugawara clan |surname nihongo=菅原氏 |image=Japanese_crest_Umebachi.svg |caption=[[:en:Mon (badge)|''Mon'']]: ''Umebachi'' |province of origin={{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} |parent house=[[Haji clan]] |founder=[[Sugawara no Furuhito]] |founding year=781 |titles= |ruled until= |dissolution= |cadet branches={{Plainlist | * [[Takatsuji clan]] * [[Gojō clan]] * [[Higashibōjō clan]] * [[Karahashi clan]] * [[Kiyo'oka clan]] * [[Kuwahara clan]] }}}} The '''Sugawara clan''' (菅原氏, ''Sugawara-uji'') was a Japanese aristocratic family claiming descent from [[Ame no Hohi]]. Founded in 781, they served the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|WD=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court}} as scholars and government officials since the clan's foundation until the early modern period when the clan divided into six branches.
In the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} served as {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}}, but the clan weakened soon after being demoted from the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. They continued to serve as heads of the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}, until Sugawara no Tamenaga became {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor}}, after which the clan reentered the upper court nobility. The clan later divided into six branches, the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], [[Kiyo'oka clan]] and [[Kuwahara clan]], all of which stayed in upper court nobility.
== Origins == [[File:Sugawara no Furuhito.jpg|thumb|[[Sugawara no Furuhito]], founder of the Sugawara clan]] The clan ancestor, [[Nomi no Sukune]], claimed descent from the male deity [[Ame no Hohi]]. Sukune belonged to the [[Haji clan]] that was in charge of burial rites. In 781, [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] became the first person to take the surname Sugawara, and thus founded the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Takigawa |first=Kōji |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1125133338 |title=Sugawarano Michizane : gakusha seijika no eikō to botsuraku |last2=滝川 |first2=幸司 |year=2019 |isbn=978-4-12-102559-3 |pages=1–2 |oclc=1125133338}}</ref> The name was taken from the Sugawara village in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.

Both Kiyotomo and Koreyoshi were scholars who served as chief experts on literature at the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}. Kiyotomo also founded a private school called Kanke Rōka, educated many students, and established ''kidendō'', the study of history, as the family business.
{{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} (845-903) was promoted to [[Junior Second Rank]] and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda}}. However, in 901, he was demoted to Provisional Governor-General of {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu}} (''Dazai no Gon-no-Sochi''), and died there. Michizane's sons were also disfavored, but in 906, his son and heir apparent Sugawara no Takami (876-913) was pardoned, and he was made head of the Imperial University.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=滝川 |first=幸司 |date=2017-09-30 |title=Sugawara no Michizaneの子息をめぐる二、三の問題 : 阿視と高視・淳茂の左遷その他 |url=https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050001337578644096 |journal=女子大國文 |language=ja |volume=161 |pages=21–56}}</ref> The descendants of Takami continued to serve as heads of the Imperial University as well as provincial governors. However, apart from [[Sugawara no Fumitoki]] (899-981), who was Junior Third Rank, and Sugawara no Sukemasa (925-1009), who was Junior Third Rank, Associate Counselor and Undersecretary of Dazaifu (''Dazai no Daini''), the clan members were not part of the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. Another notable member of the clan during this period was {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}, the author of ''{{ill|Sarashina Nikki|en|Sarashina Nikki|WD=Q217955|lt=Sarashina Nikki}}''.
In the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Sugawara no Tamenaga (1158-1246) rose up to become [[Senior Second Rank]], Associate Counselor and Head of the {{ill|Ministry of the Treasury|en|Ministry of the Treasury|WD=Q112323848|lt=Ministry of the Treasury}} (''Ōkura-kyō''). Since then, the Sugawara clan belonged to the upper court nobility for generations. Additionally, because the [[Ōe clan]], that had produced many chief experts on literature, had weakened, the Sugawara dominated the posts within the Faculty of Japanese and Chinese History.<ref name=":1" />

In the medieval {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} society, the Karahashi clan was considered to be the main line of the Sugawara clan, and dominated the role of Sugawara clan leader known as ''Kitano no Chōja''. However, after the Karahashi Arikazu murder incident, the clan weakened, and members of the Takatsuji clan, Gojō clan and Higashibōjō clan were chosen for the role instead.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Itō |first=Shingo |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/780474814 |title=Muromachi Sengokuki no kuge shakai to bunji |last2=伊藤慎吾 |publisher=Miyai Shoten |year=2012 |isbn=978-4-8382-3218-5 |edition=Shohan |pages=28–29 |oclc=780474814}}</ref>
== Clans descended from Sugawara ==
* [[Hisamatsu clan]]; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=Hotta |first=Masaatsu |date=1923 |title=寛政重脩諸家譜. 第6輯 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1082716 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |pages=889, 915, 933 |language=ja}}</ref> * [[Mimasaka Kan clan]]; a samurai family from {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}.<ref name=":2" /> * [[Maeda clan]]; descended from the Harada clan, a branch of the Mimasaka Kan clan, or from a son born to Sugawara no Michizane in [[Kyushu]].<ref name=":2" /> * Minobe family; a {{ill|Kōga-ryū|en|Kōga-ryū|WD=Q6455112|lt=Kōga-ryū}} family that claims descent from Sugawara no Atsushige.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kido |first=Masayuki |date=2009 |title=城に見る水口の中世から近世 ―美濃部氏城館・水口岡山城・水口城― |url=http://www.shiga-bunkazai.jp/download/kiyou/22_kido.pdf |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Shiga Prefectural Association for Cultural Heritage |page=2}}</ref> * {{ill|Yagyū clan|en|Yagyū clan|WD=Q2975395|lt=Yagyū clan}}; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Arai |first=Hakuseki |date=1894 |title=藩翰譜. 巻3 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1142642 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |publisher=Yoshikawa Hanshichi |page=35 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable members ==

Sukune belonged to the [[Haji clan]] that was in charge of burial rites. In 781, [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] became the first person to take the surname Sugawara, and thus founded the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Takigawa |first=Kōji |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1125133338 |title=Sugawarano Michizane : gakusha seijika no eikō to botsuraku |last2=滝川 |first2=幸司 |year=2019 |isbn=978-4-12-102559-3 |pages=1–2 |oclc=1125133338}}</ref> The name was taken from the Sugawara village in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.
== History == Sugawara no Furuhito studied {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}} and became a personal scholar (''jidoku'') to [[Emperor Kanmu]] (r. 781–806).<ref name=":0" /> Furuhito's son {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} (770-842) held the [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] of [[Junior Third Rank]], and his son {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} (812-880) was also of the same Rank and served as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor}}. Both Kiyotomo and Koreyoshi were scholars who served as chief experts on literature at the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}. Kiyotomo also founded a private school called Kanke Rōka, educated many students, and established ''kidendō'', the study of history, as the family business.
{{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} (845-903) was promoted to [[Junior Second Rank]] and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda}}. However, in 901, he was demoted to Provisional Governor-General of {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu}} (''Dazai no Gon-no-Sochi''), and died there. Michizane's sons were also disfavored, but in 906, his son and heir apparent Sugawara no Takami (876-913) was pardoned, and he was made head of the Imperial University.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=滝川 |first=幸司 |date=2017-09-30 |title=Sugawara no Michizaneの子息をめぐる二、三の問題 : 阿視と高視・淳茂の左遷その他 |url=https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050001337578644096 |journal=女子大國文 |language=ja |volume=161 |pages=21–56}}</ref> The descendants of Takami continued to serve as heads of the Imperial University as well as provincial governors. However, apart from [[Sugawara no Fumitoki]] (899-981), who was Junior Third Rank, and Sugawara no Sukemasa (925-1009), who was Junior Third Rank, Associate Counselor and Undersecretary of Dazaifu (''Dazai no Daini''), the clan members were not part of the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. Another notable member of the clan during this period was {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}, the author of ''{{ill|Sarashina Nikki|en|Sarashina Nikki|WD=Q217955|lt=Sarashina Nikki}}''.
In the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Sugawara no Tamenaga (1158-1246) rose up to become [[Senior Second Rank]], Associate Counselor and Head of the {{ill|Ministry of the Treasury|en|Ministry of the Treasury|WD=Q112323848|lt=Ministry of the Treasury}} (''Ōkura-kyō''). Since then, the Sugawara clan belonged to the upper court nobility for generations. Additionally, because the [[Ōe clan]], that had produced many chief experts on literature, had weakened, the Sugawara dominated the posts within the Faculty of Japanese and Chinese History.<ref name=":1" />
In the early modern period, the clan divided into six branches: the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], Kiyo'oka clan and Kuwahara clan, all of which were ''dōjōke''-Ranking (''hanke'') ''[[kuge]]'' families. After the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the majority of the Confucian scholars participating in the planning process of {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=era names}} were descendants of the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Tokoro |first=Isao |title=「日本年号の来歴と特色」第106回歴博フォーラム 年号と日本文化 -国立歴史民俗博物館 |url=https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/events/forum/old/f2017/pdf/106.pdf |access-date=2022-06-02 |website=National Museum of Japanese History |page=5}}</ref>
In the medieval {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} society, the Karahashi clan was considered to be the main line of the Sugawara clan, and dominated the role of Sugawara clan leader known as ''Kitano no Chōja''. However, after the Karahashi Arikazu murder incident, the clan weakened, and members of the Takatsuji clan, Gojō clan and Higashibōjō clan were chosen for the role instead.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Itō |first=Shingo |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/780474814 |title=Muromachi Sengokuki no kuge shakai to bunji |last2=伊藤慎吾 |publisher=Miyai Shoten |year=2012 |isbn=978-4-8382-3218-5 |edition=Shohan |pages=28–29 |oclc=780474814}}</ref>
== Clans descended from Sugawara ==
* [[Hisamatsu clan]]; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=Hotta |first=Masaatsu |date=1923 |title=寛政重脩諸家譜. 第6輯 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1082716 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |pages=889, 915, 933 |language=ja}}</ref> * [[Mimasaka Kan clan]]; a samurai family from {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}.<ref name=":2" /> * [[Maeda clan]]; descended from the Harada clan, a branch of the Mimasaka Kan clan, or from a son born to Sugawara no Michizane in [[Kyushu]].<ref name=":2" /> * Minobe family; a {{ill|Kōga-ryū|en|Kōga-ryū|WD=Q6455112|lt=Kōga-ryū}} family that claims descent from Sugawara no Atsushige.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kido |first=Masayuki |date=2009 |title=城に見る水口の中世から近世 ―美濃部氏城館・水口岡山城・水口城― |url=http://www.shiga-bunkazai.jp/download/kiyou/22_kido.pdf |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Shiga Prefectural Association for Cultural Heritage |page=2}}</ref> * {{ill|Yagyū clan|en|Yagyū clan|WD=Q2975395|lt=Yagyū clan}}; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Arai |first=Hakuseki |date=1894 |title=藩翰譜. 巻3 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1142642 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |publisher=Yoshikawa Hanshichi |page=35 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable members ==
* [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}
== See also ==
* {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Daigaku-ryō}} * [[Takano no Niigasa]] (Niece of Nomi no Sukune)

{{Short description|Japanese aristocratic family}} {{family name hatnote|Sugawara|lang=Japanese}} {{Japanese clan name |surname=Sugawara clan |surname nihongo=菅原氏 |image=Japanese_crest_Umebachi.svg |caption=[[:en:Mon (badge)|''Mon'']]: ''Umebachi'' |province of origin={{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} |parent house=[[Haji clan]] |founder=[[Sugawara no Furuhito]] |founding year=781 |titles= |ruled until= |dissolution= |cadet branches={{Plainlist | * [[Takatsuji clan]] * [[Gojō clan]] * [[Higashibōjō clan]] * [[Karahashi clan]] * [[Kiyo'oka clan]] * [[Kuwahara clan]] }}}} The '''Sugawara clan''' (菅原氏, ''Sugawara-uji'') was a Japanese aristocratic family claiming descent from [[Ame no Hohi]]. Founded in 781, they served the {{ill|Imperial Court in Kyoto|en|Imperial Court in Kyoto|WD=Q11680900|lt=Imperial Court}} as scholars and government officials since the clan's foundation until the early modern period when the clan divided into six branches.
In the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} served as {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}}, but the clan weakened soon after being demoted from the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. They continued to serve as heads of the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}, until Sugawara no Tamenaga became {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor}}, after which the clan reentered the upper court nobility. The clan later divided into six branches, the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], [[Kiyo'oka clan]] and [[Kuwahara clan]], all of which stayed in upper court nobility.
== Origins == [[File:Sugawara no Furuhito.jpg|thumb|[[Sugawara no Furuhito]], founder of the Sugawara clan]] The clan ancestor, [[Nomi no Sukune]], claimed descent from the male deity [[Ame no Hohi]]. Sukune belonged to the [[Haji clan]] that was in charge of burial rites. In 781, [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] became the first person to take the surname Sugawara, and thus founded the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Takigawa |first=Kōji |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1125133338 |title=Sugawarano Michizane : gakusha seijika no eikō to botsuraku |last2=滝川 |first2=幸司 |year=2019 |isbn=978-4-12-102559-3 |pages=1–2 |oclc=1125133338}}</ref> The name was taken from the Sugawara village in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}}.
== History == Sugawara no Furuhito studied {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}} and became a personal scholar (''jidoku'') to [[Emperor Kanmu]] (r. 781–806).<ref name=":0" /> Furuhito's son {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} (770-842) held the [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] of [[Junior Third Rank]], and his son {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} (812-880) was also of the same Rank and served as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor}}. Both Kiyotomo and Koreyoshi were scholars who served as chief experts on literature at the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University}}. Kiyotomo also founded a private school called Kanke Rōka, educated many students, and established ''kidendō'', the study of history, as the family business.
{{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} (845-903) was promoted to [[Junior Second Rank]] and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right}} by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda}}. However, in 901, he was demoted to Provisional Governor-General of {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu}} (''Dazai no Gon-no-Sochi''), and died there. Michizane's sons were also disfavored, but in 906, his son and heir apparent Sugawara no Takami (876-913) was pardoned, and he was made head of the Imperial University.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=滝川 |first=幸司 |date=2017-09-30 |title=Sugawara no Michizaneの子息をめぐる二、三の問題 : 阿視と高視・淳茂の左遷その他 |url=https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050001337578644096 |journal=女子大國文 |language=ja |volume=161 |pages=21–56}}</ref> The descendants of Takami continued to serve as heads of the Imperial University as well as provincial governors. However, apart from [[Sugawara no Fumitoki]] (899-981), who was Junior Third Rank, and Sugawara no Sukemasa (925-1009), who was Junior Third Rank, Associate Counselor and Undersecretary of Dazaifu (''Dazai no Daini''), the clan members were not part of the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō}}''. Another notable member of the clan during this period was {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}, the author of ''{{ill|Sarashina Nikki|en|Sarashina Nikki|WD=Q217955|lt=Sarashina Nikki}}''.
In the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, Sugawara no Tamenaga (1158-1246) rose up to become [[Senior Second Rank]], Associate Counselor and Head of the {{ill|Ministry of the Treasury|en|Ministry of the Treasury|WD=Q112323848|lt=Ministry of the Treasury}} (''Ōkura-kyō''). Since then, the Sugawara clan belonged to the upper court nobility for generations. Additionally, because the [[Ōe clan]], that had produced many chief experts on literature, had weakened, the Sugawara dominated the posts within the Faculty of Japanese and Chinese History.<ref name=":1" />
In the early modern period, the clan divided into six branches: the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], Kiyo'oka clan and Kuwahara clan, all of which were ''dōjōke''-Ranking (''hanke'') ''[[kuge]]'' families. After the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, the majority of the Confucian scholars participating in the planning process of {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=era names}} were descendants of the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Tokoro |first=Isao |title=「日本年号の来歴と特色」第106回歴博フォーラム 年号と日本文化 -国立歴史民俗博物館 |url=https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/events/forum/old/f2017/pdf/106.pdf |access-date=2022-06-02 |website=National Museum of Japanese History |page=5}}</ref>
In the medieval {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} society, the Karahashi clan was considered to be the main line of the Sugawara clan, and dominated the role of Sugawara clan leader known as ''Kitano no Chōja''. However, after the Karahashi Arikazu murder incident, the clan weakened, and members of the Takatsuji clan, Gojō clan and Higashibōjō clan were chosen for the role instead.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Itō |first=Shingo |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/780474814 |title=Muromachi Sengokuki no kuge shakai to bunji |last2=伊藤慎吾 |publisher=Miyai Shoten |year=2012 |isbn=978-4-8382-3218-5 |edition=Shohan |pages=28–29 |oclc=780474814}}</ref>
== Clans descended from Sugawara ==
* [[Hisamatsu clan]]; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=Hotta |first=Masaatsu |date=1923 |title=寛政重脩諸家譜. 第6輯 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1082716 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |pages=889, 915, 933 |language=ja}}</ref> * [[Mimasaka Kan clan]]; a samurai family from {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province}}.<ref name=":2" /> * [[Maeda clan]]; descended from the Harada clan, a branch of the Mimasaka Kan clan, or from a son born to Sugawara no Michizane in [[Kyushu]].<ref name=":2" /> * Minobe family; a {{ill|Kōga-ryū|en|Kōga-ryū|WD=Q6455112|lt=Kōga-ryū}} family that claims descent from Sugawara no Atsushige.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kido |first=Masayuki |date=2009 |title=城に見る水口の中世から近世 ―美濃部氏城館・水口岡山城・水口城― |url=http://www.shiga-bunkazai.jp/download/kiyou/22_kido.pdf |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Shiga Prefectural Association for Cultural Heritage |page=2}}</ref> * {{ill|Yagyū clan|en|Yagyū clan|WD=Q2975395|lt=Yagyū clan}}; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Arai |first=Hakuseki |date=1894 |title=藩翰譜. 巻3 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1142642 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |publisher=Yoshikawa Hanshichi |page=35 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable members ==
* [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter}}

In 781, [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] became the first person to take the surname Sugawara, and thus founded the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Takigawa |first=Kōji |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1125133338 |title=Sugawarano Michizane : gakusha seijika no eikō to botsuraku |last2=滝川 |first2=幸司 |year=2019 |isbn=978-4-12-102559-3 |pages=1–2 |oclc=1125133338}}</ref> The name was taken from the Sugawara village in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}}.
== History == Sugawara no Furuhito studied {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}} and became a personal scholar (''jidoku'') to [[Emperor Kanmu]] (r. 781–806).<ref name=":0" /> Furuhito's son {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo|1=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} (770-842) held the [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|court Rank]] of [[Junior Third Rank]], and his son {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi|1=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} (812-880) was also of the same Rank and served as {{ill|Sangi (Japan)|en|Sangi (Japan)|WD=Q1133663|lt=Associate Counselor|1=Sangi (Japan)}}. Both Kiyotomo and Koreyoshi were scholars who served as chief experts on literature at the {{ill|Daigaku-ryō|en|Daigaku-ryō|WD=Q3012048|lt=Imperial University|1=Daigaku-ryō}}. Kiyotomo also founded a private school called Kanke Rōka, educated many students, and established ''kidendō'', the study of history, as the family business.
{{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} (845-903) was promoted to [[Junior Second Rank]] and {{ill|Minister of the Right|en|Minister of the Right|WD=Q1351174|lt=Minister of the Right|1=Minister of the Right}} by {{ill|Emperor Uda|en|Emperor Uda|WD=Q349267|lt=Emperor Uda|1=Emperor Uda}}. However, in 901, he was demoted to Provisional Governor-General of {{ill|Dazaifu (government)|en|Dazaifu (government)|WD=Q144845|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu (government)}} (''Dazai no Gon-no-Sochi''), and died there. Michizane's sons were also disfavored, but in 906, his son and heir apparent Sugawara no Takami (876-913) was pardoned, and he was made head of the Imperial University.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=滝川 |first=幸司 |date=2017-09-30 |title=Sugawara no Michizaneの子息をめぐる二、三の問題 : 阿視と高視・淳茂の左遷その他 |url=https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1050001337578644096 |journal=女子大國文 |language=ja |volume=161 |pages=21–56}}</ref> The descendants of Takami continued to serve as heads of the Imperial University as well as provincial governors. However, apart from [[Sugawara no Fumitoki]] (899-981), who was Junior Third Rank, and Sugawara no Sukemasa (925-1009), who was Junior Third Rank, Associate Counselor and Undersecretary of Dazaifu (''Dazai no Daini''), the clan members were not part of the upper court nobility, ''{{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|WD=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō|1=Kugyō}}''. Another notable member of the clan during this period was {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter|1=Takasue's daughter}}, the author of ''{{ill|Sarashina Nikki|en|Sarashina Nikki|WD=Q217955|lt=Sarashina Nikki|1=Sarashina Nikki}}''.
In the early {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, Sugawara no Tamenaga (1158-1246) rose up to become [[Senior Second Rank]], Associate Counselor and Head of the {{ill|Ministry of the Treasury|en|Ministry of the Treasury|WD=Q112323848|lt=Ministry of the Treasury|1=Ministry of the Treasury}} (''Ōkura-kyō''). Since then, the Sugawara clan belonged to the upper court nobility for generations. Additionally, because the [[Ōe clan]], that had produced many chief experts on literature, had weakened, the Sugawara dominated the posts within the Faculty of Japanese and Chinese History.<ref name=":1" />
In the early modern period, the clan divided into six branches: the [[Takatsuji clan]], [[Gojō clan]], [[Higashibōjō clan]], [[Karahashi clan]], Kiyo'oka clan and Kuwahara clan, all of which were ''dōjōke''-Ranking (''hanke'') ''[[kuge]]'' families. After the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the majority of the Confucian scholars participating in the planning process of {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=era names|1=Japanese era name}} were descendants of the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=Tokoro |first=Isao |title=「日本年号の来歴と特色」第106回歴博フォーラム 年号と日本文化 -国立歴史民俗博物館 |url=https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/events/forum/old/f2017/pdf/106.pdf |access-date=2022-06-02 |website=National Museum of Japanese History |page=5}}</ref>
In the medieval {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} society, the Karahashi clan was considered to be the main line of the Sugawara clan, and dominated the role of Sugawara clan leader known as ''Kitano no Chōja''. However, after the Karahashi Arikazu murder incident, the clan weakened, and members of the Takatsuji clan, Gojō clan and Higashibōjō clan were chosen for the role instead.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Itō |first=Shingo |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/780474814 |title=Muromachi Sengokuki no kuge shakai to bunji |last2=伊藤慎吾 |publisher=Miyai Shoten |year=2012 |isbn=978-4-8382-3218-5 |edition=Shohan |pages=28–29 |oclc=780474814}}</ref>
== Clans descended from Sugawara ==
* [[Hisamatsu clan]]; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=Hotta |first=Masaatsu |date=1923 |title=寛政重脩諸家譜. 第6輯 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1082716 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |pages=889, 915, 933 |language=ja}}</ref> * [[Mimasaka Kan clan]]; a samurai family from {{ill|Mimasaka Province|en|Mimasaka Province|WD=Q936036|lt=Mimasaka Province|1=Mimasaka Province}}.<ref name=":2" /> * [[Maeda clan]]; descended from the Harada clan, a branch of the Mimasaka Kan clan, or from a son born to Sugawara no Michizane in [[Kyushu]].<ref name=":2" /> * Minobe family; a {{ill|Kōga-ryū|en|Kōga-ryū|WD=Q6455112|lt=Kōga-ryū|1=Kōga-ryū}} family that claims descent from Sugawara no Atsushige.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kido |first=Masayuki |date=2009 |title=城に見る水口の中世から近世 ―美濃部氏城館・水口岡山城・水口城― |url=http://www.shiga-bunkazai.jp/download/kiyou/22_kido.pdf |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=Shiga Prefectural Association for Cultural Heritage |page=2}}</ref> * {{ill|Yagyū clan|en|Yagyū clan|WD=Q2975395|lt=Yagyū clan|1=Yagyū clan}}; descended from the Sugawara clan.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Arai |first=Hakuseki |date=1894 |title=藩翰譜. 巻3 - 国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1142642 |access-date=2022-06-03 |website=National Diet Library |publisher=Yoshikawa Hanshichi |page=35 |language=ja}}</ref>
== Notable members ==
* [[Sugawara no Furuhito]] * {{ill|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|en|Sugawara no Kiyotomo|WD=Q3503077|lt=Sugawara no Kiyotomo|1=Sugawara no Kiyotomo}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|en|Sugawara no Koreyoshi|WD=Q11619187|lt=Sugawara no Koreyoshi|1=Sugawara no Koreyoshi}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|Takasue's daughter|en|Takasue's daughter|WD=Q461024|lt=Sugawara no Takasue's daughter|1=Takasue's daughter}}
